content:
# Disagreement Log — Leviticus 7
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 60v–61r

---

## Summary

Leviticus 7 produced disagreements across a substantial portion of its 38 verses. The primary fault lines are: (1) PNEUMA's use of "procedure" for torah vs. EMET/LOGOS "instruction"; (2) PNEUMA's use of "purification offering" for chattat, violating project convention; (3) PNEUMA's use of "person" / inclusive gender pronouns vs. EMET/LOGOS more literal renderings; (4) structural and word-order differences in numerous verses; (5) LOGOS using "people of Israel" / PNEUMA using "Israelites" vs. EMET's "sons of Israel"; and (6) scattered lexical differences in nearly every verse. Every difference is logged below.

---

### Verse 1 — Opening formula: "instruction" vs. "procedure" / title framing

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "And this is the torah [instruction] of the guilt offering [zot torat ha-asham] — it is most holy [qodesh qodashim hu]:" |
| LOGOS | "And this is the instruction of the guilt offering; it is most holy." |
| PNEUMA | "This is the procedure for the guilt offering. It is most holy." |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "And this is the instruction of the guilt offering — it is most holy." EMET and LOGOS agree on "instruction" for torah, which is the project convention. PNEUMA's "procedure" is more accessible but abandons the established term. The opening conjunction "And" (EMET/LOGOS) is retained — the chapter continues the legal block of ch. 6 and the waw-consecutive is present. PNEUMA drops the opening "And," which is a minor structural difference also logged here. EMET's bracketed transliterations are omitted in the final text per the consensus format. The semicolon vs. em-dash difference between LOGOS and EMET is resolved in favor of the em-dash, which connects the most-holy declaration more naturally to the preceding identification.

---

### Verse 2 — Blood application verb: "dash" / "thrown" / "splashed"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "...its blood he shall dash against the altar on all sides." |
| LOGOS | "...its blood shall be thrown against the altar on all sides." |
| PNEUMA | "...its blood shall be splashed against all sides of the altar." |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "...its blood shall be thrown against the altar on all sides." LOGOS's "thrown" best captures zaraq (a forceful tossing gesture). EMET's "dash" is accurate but slightly archaic. PNEUMA's "splash" is too casual and implies liquid scattering without directed force. LOGOS's passive "shall be thrown" is also adopted (vs. EMET's active "he shall dash"), which matches the impersonal legal register of the surrounding instructions. Note also: PNEUMA restructures the phrase as "against all sides of the altar" (moving the altar to the end), while EMET and LOGOS use "against the altar on all sides." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS word order, which is closer to the Hebrew (al-ha-mizbeach saviv).

Also: PNEUMA combines vv. 1–6 into a single paragraph without verse-by-verse breaks in the body text, representing a structural difference from EMET and LOGOS. SOPHIA renders each verse separately per project format.

---

### Verse 3 — "offer" / "offer" / "present"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "And all its fat he shall offer from it: the fat tail and the fat that covers the entrails," |
| LOGOS | "And all its fat he shall offer from it: the fat tail and the fat that covers the entrails," |
| PNEUMA | "All of its fat is to be presented as an offering: the fat tail, the fat that covers the entrails," |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "And all its fat he shall offer from it: the fat tail and the fat that covers the entrails," EMET and LOGOS are identical here. PNEUMA differs in three ways: drops the opening "And," uses passive construction "is to be presented as an offering" rather than "he shall offer from it," and uses a comma after "entrails" rather than continuing to v. 4. SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS for the active construction and the conjunction, which are closer to the MT.

---

### Verse 4 — Anatomical description: "by the flanks" / "at the loins" / "near the loins"; liver lobe clause structure

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "and the two kidneys and the fat that is on them which is by the flanks, and the lobe on the liver — upon the kidneys he shall remove it." |
| LOGOS | "and the two kidneys with the fat that is on them at the loins, and the lobe of the liver that he shall remove with the kidneys." |
| PNEUMA | "the two kidneys with the fat on them near the loins, and the lobe of the liver — which is to be removed along with the kidneys." |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "and the two kidneys with the fat that is on them at the loins, and the lobe of the liver — which he shall remove along with the kidneys." Three layers of difference: (1) "by the flanks" (EMET, ha-kesalim) vs. "at the loins" (LOGOS) vs. "near the loins" (PNEUMA) — SOPHIA adopts "at the loins," which is anatomically precise and more idiomatic than "flanks." (2) The fat clause: EMET's "the fat that is on them" with a separate "which is by the flanks" relative clause vs. LOGOS/PNEUMA's integrated "with the fat that is on them at the loins." SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA for smoother syntax. (3) The liver lobe clause: EMET renders "upon the kidneys he shall remove it" (preserving the Hebrew al-ha-kelayot yesirenah order); LOGOS renders "that he shall remove with the kidneys" (relative clause); PNEUMA uses "which is to be removed along with the kidneys" (passive). SOPHIA adopts a hybrid of EMET and LOGOS: "— which he shall remove along with the kidneys," combining PNEUMA's relative clause structure with LOGOS's active agent and EMET's conjunction.

---

### Verse 5 — Minimal differences

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "And the priest shall burn them on the altar as a fire offering [isheh] to the LORD — it is a guilt offering [asham hu]." |
| LOGOS | "And the priest shall burn them on the altar as a fire offering to the LORD; it is a guilt offering." |
| PNEUMA | "The priest shall burn them on the altar as a fire-offering to the LORD. It is a guilt offering." |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "And the priest shall burn them on the altar as a fire offering to the LORD; it is a guilt offering." The three agents agree substantially. Differences: (1) PNEUMA drops the opening "And"; SOPHIA retains it (consistent with the legal enumeration). (2) PNEUMA hyphenates "fire-offering"; SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS without hyphen ("fire offering"), consistent with project convention. (3) EMET uses an em-dash before "it is a guilt offering"; LOGOS uses a semicolon; PNEUMA uses a period. SOPHIA follows LOGOS with the semicolon — the declaration is connected to but distinct from the burning instruction.

---

### Verse 6 — "may eat it" / "may eat of it" / permission vs. obligation phrasing

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "Every male among the priests shall eat it; in a holy place it shall be eaten — it is most holy." |
| LOGOS | "Every male among the priests may eat of it. It shall be eaten in a holy place; it is most holy." |
| PNEUMA | "Every male among the priests may eat it. It is to be eaten in a holy place — it is most holy." |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "Every male among the priests may eat of it. It shall be eaten in a holy place — it is most holy." Three differences: (1) "shall eat" (EMET) vs. "may eat" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) — SOPHIA adopts "may eat," which renders the permissive imperfect more naturally for an entitlement regulation. (2) "of it" (LOGOS) vs. no partitive (EMET/PNEUMA) — SOPHIA retains "of it" (LOGOS), which the Hebrew supports. (3) Sentence-break placement: EMET uses a semicolon to join the clauses; LOGOS uses a full stop after "of it"; PNEUMA also uses a full stop. SOPHIA uses a full stop to follow the logical break, then an em-dash before "it is most holy" to mark the declaratory climax.

---

### Verse 7 — Chattat as "sin offering" vs. "purification offering"; comparison order; "have it" vs. "shall have it" vs. "belongs to him"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "Like the sin offering, so is the guilt offering; one law [torah achat] is for them — the priest who makes atonement with it, his it shall be." |
| LOGOS | "The guilt offering is like the sin offering; there is one instruction for them. The priest who makes atonement with it shall have it." |
| PNEUMA | "The guilt offering is handled like the purification offering: the same procedure applies to both. The meat belongs to the priest who makes atonement with it." |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "The guilt offering is like the sin offering; there is one instruction for them. The priest who makes atonement with it shall have it." Four layers of difference: (1) PNEUMA renders chattat as "purification offering" — this violates the project-wide key term convention (chattat = "sin offering"). SOPHIA rules against PNEUMA on this point categorically. (2) Comparison order: EMET has "Like the sin offering, so is the guilt offering" (following the Hebrew ka-chattat ka-asham word order); LOGOS and PNEUMA lead with the guilt offering as subject. SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA — this chapter is primarily about the guilt offering, so making it the subject is contextually fitting. (3) PNEUMA renders torah as "procedure" and uses "the same procedure applies to both" rather than "one instruction for them." SOPHIA rules for "one instruction for them" per project convention. (4) The priestly entitlement clause: EMET has "his it shall be" (wooden literal); LOGOS has "shall have it"; PNEUMA has "belongs to the priest who." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "shall have it" — clear, idiomatic, accurate.

---

### Verse 8 — "offers" / "offers" / "presents"; "that priest shall have for himself" / EMET emphatic structure

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "And the priest who offers [ha-makriv] any man's burnt offering — the skin of the burnt offering that he has offered, it shall be his." |
| LOGOS | "And the priest who offers any man's burnt offering, that priest shall have for himself the skin of the burnt offering that he has offered." |
| PNEUMA | "The priest who presents a man's burnt offering may keep the hide of that burnt offering for himself." |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "And the priest who offers any man's burnt offering — that priest shall have for himself the skin of the burnt offering that he has offered." Differences: (1) PNEUMA drops "And"; SOPHIA retains it. (2) PNEUMA uses "presents" for the priestly action; EMET and LOGOS use "offers." SOPHIA follows "offers" — the established project term for hiqriv in priestly contexts. (3) PNEUMA uses "hide" for or ha-olah; EMET and LOGOS use "skin." SOPHIA follows "skin" — more dignified in a sacrificial-legal context and the established rendering. (4) PNEUMA uses "may keep... for himself" which collapses the Hebrew's repeated "he has offered" into the shorter form. SOPHIA follows LOGOS's fuller rendering that preserves the offered-skin clause. (5) EMET structures the sentence with a fronted object ("the skin... it shall be his") — a literal but awkward rendering. SOPHIA follows LOGOS's more natural English syntax.

---

### Verse 9 — "prepared in a pan or on a griddle" / "made on a pan or on a griddle" / "prepared on a griddle or in a pan"; "belongs to" / "shall belong"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "And every grain offering baked in the oven and every one made on a pan or on a griddle — to the priest who offers it it shall belong." |
| LOGOS | "And every grain offering baked in an oven and every grain offering prepared in a pan or on a griddle shall belong to the priest who offers it." |
| PNEUMA | "Every grain offering baked in an oven, and every grain offering prepared on a griddle or in a pan, belongs to the priest who presents it." |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "And every grain offering baked in an oven and every grain offering prepared on a pan or on a griddle shall belong to the priest who offers it." Differences: (1) EMET uses "the oven" (definite); LOGOS and PNEUMA use "an oven" (indefinite). SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA — the general legal register favors the indefinite article. (2) EMET uses "made on a pan or on a griddle"; LOGOS uses "prepared in a pan or on a griddle"; PNEUMA reverses to "on a griddle or in a pan." SOPHIA uses "on a pan or on a griddle," restoring the MT order (machabat then machashat) and using "on" for both (the Hebrew uses the same preposition for both). (3) EMET has awkward inverted word order "to the priest who offers it it shall belong"; LOGOS and PNEUMA have natural order. SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA. (4) PNEUMA uses "presents" where EMET/LOGOS use "offers." SOPHIA follows "offers."

---

### Verse 10 — "one as well as another" / "one as much as another" / "equally to all"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "And every grain offering mixed with oil or dry — to all the sons of Aaron it shall belong, one as well as another." |
| LOGOS | "And every grain offering mixed with oil or dry shall belong to all the sons of Aaron, one as much as another." |
| PNEUMA | "But every grain offering mixed with oil or dry belongs equally to all of Aaron's sons." |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "And every grain offering mixed with oil or dry shall belong to all the sons of Aaron, one as much as another." Differences: (1) PNEUMA uses "But" (contrastive); EMET and LOGOS use "And." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS — the Hebrew uses waw here, and while an implied contrast with v. 9 is present, "But" is interpretive. (2) EMET renders ish ke-achiv as "one as well as another"; LOGOS renders "one as much as another"; PNEUMA collapses the idiom into "equally." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "one as much as another" preserves the idiomatic pair from the Hebrew and signals the equal distribution without interpreting it away. (3) PNEUMA uses "Aaron's sons" rather than "the sons of Aaron." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: "the sons of Aaron" is the established form in this project.

---

### Verse 11 — "one may offer" / "one may offer" / "someone presents"; opening formula differences

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "And this is the torah [instruction] of the sacrifice of peace offerings [zot torat zevach ha-shelamim] that one shall offer [asher yaqriv] to the LORD:" |
| LOGOS | "And this is the instruction of the sacrifice of peace offerings that one may offer to the LORD." |
| PNEUMA | "This is the procedure for the peace offering that someone presents to the LORD:" |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "And this is the instruction of the sacrifice of peace offerings that one may offer to the LORD:" Three differences: (1) PNEUMA again uses "procedure" for torah; SOPHIA follows "instruction." (2) PNEUMA uses "peace offering" (singular, shortened); EMET and LOGOS use "sacrifice of peace offerings" (zevach ha-shelamim). SOPHIA retains the full technical designation. (3) "shall offer" (EMET) vs. "may offer" (LOGOS) vs. "presents" (PNEUMA) — SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "may offer" renders yaqriv as a general-purpose imperfect, which is appropriate for the opening of a legal block about voluntary offerings. (4) PNEUMA drops the opening "And" and the colon. SOPHIA retains both.

---

### Verse 12 — "unleavened wafers smeared with oil" / "spread with oil" / "spread with oil"; "well mixed with oil" / "well kneaded"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "If he offers it for a thanksgiving he shall offer along with the sacrifice of thanksgiving unleavened loaves mixed with oil, and unleavened wafers spread with oil, and well-soaked loaves of fine flour mixed with oil." |
| LOGOS | "If he offers it as a thanksgiving, then he shall offer with the sacrifice of thanksgiving unleavened loaves mixed with oil, unleavened wafers smeared with oil, and loaves of fine flour well mixed with oil." |
| PNEUMA | "If he brings it as a thanksgiving offering, along with the thanksgiving sacrifice he shall present: unleavened loaves mixed with oil, unleavened wafers spread with oil, and loaves of fine flour mixed with oil — well kneaded." |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "If he offers it as a thanksgiving offering, then he shall offer with the sacrifice of thanksgiving: unleavened loaves mixed with oil, unleavened wafers smeared with oil, and loaves of fine flour well mixed with oil." Multiple differences: (1) "as a thanksgiving" (LOGOS) vs. "for a thanksgiving" (EMET) vs. "as a thanksgiving offering" (PNEUMA) — SOPHIA follows PNEUMA in adding "offering" to make the technical term clearer, but adopts "as" from LOGOS/PNEUMA over EMET's "for." (2) The second bread type: "spread with oil" (EMET/PNEUMA) vs. "smeared with oil" (LOGOS). The Hebrew meshuchim comes from mashach (to smear/anoint/spread). SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "smeared" is more precise (meshuchim refers to coating the surface, which "smeared" captures better than the more neutral "spread"). (3) The third bread type: EMET renders "well-soaked loaves of fine flour mixed with oil" (reading murbekheth as "soaked/softened"); LOGOS renders "loaves of fine flour well mixed with oil" (reading murbekheth as "kneaded/well-mixed"); PNEUMA renders "loaves of fine flour mixed with oil — well kneaded." The hapax murbekheth most likely means "thoroughly kneaded/worked" (cf. Lev 6:21 where the same word appears for the high priest's daily grain offering). SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "loaves of fine flour well mixed with oil." (4) Conjunctions before each bread type: EMET uses "and... and... and" between all three; LOGOS drops the conjunctions for a list; PNEUMA uses a colon then comma-separated list. SOPHIA follows LOGOS: a colon after "thanksgiving" then the three types as a comma-separated list.

---

### Verse 13 — Clause order and "with loaves of leavened bread" placement

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "With loaves of leavened bread he shall bring his offering alongside the sacrifice of his peace offering for thanksgiving." |
| LOGOS | "With the sacrifice of his thanksgiving peace offerings he shall bring his offering with loaves of leavened bread." |
| PNEUMA | "He shall present this offering along with loaves of leavened bread alongside his thanksgiving peace offering." |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "He shall bring his offering with loaves of leavened bread alongside the sacrifice of his thanksgiving peace offering." Differences: (1) Clause order: EMET fronts "With loaves of leavened bread" (following the Hebrew word order al-challot lechem chametz yaqriv qorbano); LOGOS and PNEUMA put the subject first. SOPHIA follows a middle path: subjects first but preserves "with loaves of leavened bread" as a companion phrase, following the sense of the Hebrew. (2) "shall bring" (EMET/SOPHIA) vs. "he shall bring" (LOGOS positions object first) vs. "He shall present" (PNEUMA). SOPHIA follows "shall bring" with subject restored. (3) PNEUMA uses "this offering" which is interpretive; EMET and LOGOS use "his offering." SOPHIA follows "his offering." (4) The peace offering qualifier: "his peace offering for thanksgiving" (EMET) vs. "his thanksgiving peace offerings" (LOGOS) vs. "his thanksgiving peace offering" (PNEUMA). SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA: "his thanksgiving peace offering" — the Hebrew zevach todah shelamav can be read as a construct chain ("the sacrifice of thanksgiving, his peace offerings"), and "thanksgiving peace offering" is the most natural English compound.

---

### Verse 14 — "one loaf from each offering" / "one from each offering" / "one piece"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "And from it he shall offer one from each offering as a contribution [terumah] to the LORD — to the priest who dashes the blood of the peace offerings, his it shall be." |
| LOGOS | "And from it he shall offer one loaf from each offering, as a contribution to the LORD; it shall belong to the priest who throws the blood of the peace offerings." |
| PNEUMA | "From each offering he shall bring one piece as a contribution to the LORD; it belongs to the priest who splashes the blood of the peace offering." |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "And from it he shall offer one from each offering as a contribution to the LORD — it shall belong to the priest who throws the blood of the peace offerings." Differences: (1) LOGOS adds "loaf" (clarifying what the "one" refers to); PNEUMA adds "piece." The Hebrew echad mi-kol-qorban means "one from each offering" without specifying the form — context makes clear it is one loaf (from the bread types of v. 12-13). SOPHIA omits the interpretive addition, following EMET: "one from each offering." (2) "dashes" (EMET) vs. "throws" (LOGOS) vs. "splashes" (PNEUMA) for zoreh — the same verb as zaraq but here in the Hiphil. SOPHIA follows "throws" (LOGOS), consistent with the ruling at v. 2 for zaraq; "dashes" is slightly archaic, "splashes" too casual. (3) EMET has the awkward inverted "his it shall be." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "it shall belong to the priest who throws the blood." (4) PNEUMA drops the opening "And"; SOPHIA retains it.

---

### Verse 15 — "shall be eaten" / "shall be eaten" / "must be eaten"; "he shall not leave" / "He shall not leave" / "none of it may be kept"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "And the flesh of the sacrifice of his thanksgiving peace offering shall be eaten on the day of his offering — he shall not leave any of it until morning." |
| LOGOS | "And the flesh of the sacrifice of his thanksgiving peace offerings shall be eaten on the day of his offering. He shall not leave any of it until the morning." |
| PNEUMA | "The meat of the thanksgiving peace offering must be eaten on the day it is offered — none of it may be kept until morning." |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "And the flesh of the sacrifice of his thanksgiving peace offering shall be eaten on the day of his offering — he shall not leave any of it until morning." Differences: (1) PNEUMA uses "meat" for basar; EMET and LOGOS use "flesh." SOPHIA follows "flesh" — the established project term for basar in sacrificial contexts. (2) PNEUMA uses "must be eaten" (stronger obligation); EMET and LOGOS use "shall be eaten." SOPHIA follows "shall be eaten" — the legal imperfect is aptly rendered by "shall." (3) PNEUMA shortens to "the thanksgiving peace offering" and drops "the sacrifice of his"; EMET and LOGOS preserve the full "the sacrifice of his thanksgiving peace offering." SOPHIA preserves the full form. (4) LOGOS adds "the" before "morning" ("until the morning"); EMET does not ("until morning"); PNEUMA omits both. SOPHIA follows EMET: "until morning" — the Hebrew ad-boqer lacks the article and the morning is general, not a specific morning.

---

### Verse 16 — "vow or a freewill offering" vs. "vow offering or a freewill offering"; "also be eaten" (EMET) vs. "shall be eaten" (LOGOS)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "But if the sacrifice of his offering is a vow or a freewill offering, on the day he offers his sacrifice it shall be eaten, and on the next day what remains of it shall also be eaten." |
| LOGOS | "But if the sacrifice of his offering is a vow or a freewill offering, it shall be eaten on the day that he offers his sacrifice, and on the next day what remains of it shall be eaten." |
| PNEUMA | "If his offering is a vow offering or a freewill offering, it shall be eaten on the day he presents his sacrifice, and whatever remains may be eaten the next day." |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "But if the sacrifice of his offering is a vow or a freewill offering, it shall be eaten on the day that he offers his sacrifice, and on the next day what remains of it shall also be eaten." Differences: (1) PNEUMA drops "But" and "the sacrifice of his offering," shortening to "his offering." SOPHIA retains "But if the sacrifice of his offering" — the full form is in the Hebrew (ve-im neder o nedavah zivcho). (2) "vow or a freewill offering" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "vow offering or a freewill offering" (PNEUMA). SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: "vow" is the established rendering of neder without the added gloss "offering." (3) "on the day he offers" (EMET, PNEUMA) vs. "on the day that he offers" (LOGOS). SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "that" is a natural relative conjunction here. (4) EMET adds "also" ("shall also be eaten"); LOGOS omits "also." The Hebrew gam (also) is present in the MT. SOPHIA retains "also" — it is in the text. (5) PNEUMA uses "may be eaten" (permissive) for the next-day eating; EMET and LOGOS use "shall be eaten" (obligative). The Hebrew is an imperfect (ye'akhel) without strong modal marking — the context implies permission rather than command. SOPHIA follows LOGOS's "shall be eaten" as the more formal and consistent rendering of the imperfect in this legal context.

---

### Verse 17 — "burned with fire" / "burned up with fire" / "burned in the fire"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "And what remains of the flesh of the sacrifice on the third day shall be burned with fire." |
| LOGOS | "But what remains of the flesh of the sacrifice on the third day shall be burned up with fire." |
| PNEUMA | "But anything left of the sacrifice's meat on the third day must be burned in the fire." |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "But what remains of the flesh of the sacrifice on the third day shall be burned with fire." Differences: (1) EMET uses "And"; LOGOS and PNEUMA use "But" — the Hebrew waw here is contrastive (contrasting the permitted eating of v. 16 with the prohibition of v. 17). SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA: "But." (2) "burned with fire" (EMET) vs. "burned up with fire" (LOGOS) vs. "burned in the fire" (PNEUMA). SOPHIA follows EMET: "burned with fire" — ba-esh yissaref is literally "in fire it shall be burned" but "burned with fire" is the established project rendering. LOGOS's "burned up" adds completeness; PNEUMA's "in the fire" localizes unnecessarily. (3) PNEUMA uses "meat" for basar; SOPHIA follows "flesh." (4) PNEUMA changes "what remains of the flesh of the sacrifice" to "anything left of the sacrifice's meat," dropping "flesh" and restructuring the genitive. SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS.

---

### Verse 18 — "piggul" treatment; "abomination" / "offensive" / transliterated; "bear his iniquity" / "bear his own guilt"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "And if any of the flesh of the sacrifice of his peace offering is ever eaten on the third day, it shall not be accepted — he who offers it shall not have it reckoned to him; it shall be a piggul [rejected thing / foul thing], and the life that eats of it shall bear her iniquity." |
| LOGOS | "If any of the flesh of the sacrifice of his peace offerings is eaten on the third day, he who offers it shall not be accepted, and it shall not be credited to him. It shall be an abomination, and the person who eats of it shall bear his iniquity." |
| PNEUMA | "If any of the meat from the peace offering is eaten on the third day, the person who brought it will not be accepted — it will not be credited to him. It has become offensive, and the person who eats it will bear his own guilt." |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "If any of the flesh of the sacrifice of his peace offerings is eaten on the third day, it shall not be accepted — he who offers it shall not have it credited to him. It shall be a piggul, and the person who eats of it shall bear his iniquity." Multiple differences: (1) EMET opens with "And if"; LOGOS and PNEUMA use "If." SOPHIA drops the opening "And" here — the shift to a new legal sub-case is clearer without it. (2) EMET's "ever eaten" adds emphasis not in the MT; LOGOS and PNEUMA simply "is eaten." SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA. (3) The clause order: EMET puts "it shall not be accepted" first, then "shall not have it reckoned to him"; LOGOS puts "he who offers it shall not be accepted" first; PNEUMA follows LOGOS order. The Hebrew has lo yeratseb then lo yechashev lo. SOPHIA renders "it shall not be accepted — he who offers it shall not have it credited to him," following the MT clause order while using an em-dash for the connection. (4) Piggul rendering: EMET transliterates with bracketed gloss; LOGOS renders "abomination"; PNEUMA renders "offensive." SOPHIA rules to retain the transliterated "piggul" without a gloss — it is a technical priestly term with a precise meaning defined by its context, and the established project approach (cf. massebah) supports retaining rare technical terms in their Hebrew form. (5) "reckoned" (EMET) vs. "credited" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) for yechashev. SOPHIA follows "credited" — in an offerings context, the accounting metaphor of "credited to his account" is appropriate and idiomatic. (6) "the life that eats" (EMET) vs. "the person who eats" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA: "person" is established for nefesh in legal contexts. (7) "bear her iniquity" (EMET, feminine pronoun for nefesh) vs. "bear his iniquity" (LOGOS) vs. "bear his own guilt" (PNEUMA). SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "bear his iniquity" — the pronoun refers to the generic "person" (using masculine as grammatically unmarked); "guilt" (PNEUMA) collapses the specific Hebrew distinction between avon (iniquity) and asham (guilt). (8) PNEUMA uses "the person who brought it" for ha-maqrib oto (the one who offers it), introducing "brought" where EMET/LOGOS use "offers." SOPHIA follows "he who offers it."

### Verse 19 — "Flesh" vs. "Meat"; "clean person" vs. "clean" / "ceremonially clean"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "And the flesh that touches any unclean thing shall not be eaten; it shall be burned with fire; and as for the flesh, every clean person may eat the flesh." |
| LOGOS | "Flesh that touches any unclean thing shall not be eaten. It shall be burned up with fire. As for the flesh, everyone who is clean may eat the flesh." |
| PNEUMA | "Meat that has touched anything ritually unclean must not be eaten; it must be burned in the fire. As for other meat, anyone who is ceremonially clean may eat it." |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "Flesh that touches any unclean thing shall not be eaten; it shall be burned with fire. As for the flesh, every clean person may eat the flesh." Differences: (1) PNEUMA uses "meat" for basar; SOPHIA follows "flesh." (2) EMET opens with "And the flesh"; LOGOS and PNEUMA drop "And." SOPHIA drops "And" — the verse begins a new legal rule. (3) "ritually unclean" (PNEUMA) vs. "unclean" (EMET/LOGOS). SOPHIA uses "unclean" — adding "ritually" is interpretive expansion not in the Hebrew. (4) "must not be eaten" (PNEUMA) vs. "shall not be eaten" (EMET/LOGOS). SOPHIA follows "shall not be eaten." (5) "burned up with fire" (LOGOS) vs. "burned with fire" (EMET) vs. "burned in the fire" (PNEUMA). SOPHIA follows EMET: "burned with fire." (6) "As for other meat" (PNEUMA) adds "other," which is interpretive; EMET and LOGOS say "As for the flesh." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS. (7) "every clean person" (EMET) vs. "everyone who is clean" (LOGOS) vs. "anyone who is ceremonially clean" (PNEUMA). SOPHIA follows EMET: "every clean person" — "ceremonially clean" (PNEUMA) adds an interpretive modifier; "everyone who is clean" (LOGOS) is slightly more verbose.

### Verse 20 — "life" / "person" for nefesh; "his people" vs. "her peoples" (gender of nefesh pronoun)

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "But the life who eats flesh from the sacrifice of peace offerings that belongs to the LORD while his uncleanness is upon him — that life shall be cut off from her peoples." |
| LOGOS | "But the person who eats of the flesh of the sacrifice of peace offerings that belong to the LORD while his uncleanness is upon him, that person shall be cut off from his people." |
| PNEUMA | "But if a person eats meat from the LORD's peace offering while in a state of ritual impurity, that person shall be cut off from their people." |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "But the person who eats of the flesh of the sacrifice of peace offerings that belongs to the LORD while his uncleanness is upon him — that person shall be cut off from his people." Differences: (1) EMET uses "life" for nefesh; LOGOS and PNEUMA use "person." SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA: "person." (2) EMET preserves the Hebrew feminine pronoun for nefesh ("her peoples"); LOGOS uses masculine ("his people"); PNEUMA uses gender-neutral plural ("their people"). SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "his people" — grammatically conventional for the masculine-unmarked generic and avoids EMET's anachronistic grammatical inconsistency (using "his uncleanness" but "her peoples"). (3) PNEUMA shortens the peace offering designation to "the LORD's peace offering"; EMET and LOGOS preserve "the sacrifice of peace offerings that belongs to the LORD." SOPHIA retains the full designation (EMET/LOGOS). (4) PNEUMA uses "in a state of ritual impurity" for ve-tum'ato alav; EMET and LOGOS use "while his uncleanness is upon him." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS — it is more literal and avoids the interpretive "ritual" addition. (5) PNEUMA restructures to "if a person eats"; EMET and LOGOS use "who eats... that person." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS relative clause structure, which matches the Hebrew participle construction.

| LOGOS | "And if anyone touches an unclean thing — whether the uncleanness of man or an unclean beast or any unclean detestable creature — and then eats some of the flesh of the sacrifice of peace offerings that belong to the LORD, that person shall be cut off from his people." |

Differences: (1) EMET uses "a life"; LOGOS uses "anyone"; PNEUMA uses "a person." SOPHIA follows "anyone" (LOGOS) — the most natural English rendering of the legal generalization. (5) PNEUMA drops "the sacrifice of peace offerings" and shortens to "the LORD's peace offering"; SOPHIA retains the full form. (6) EMET: "her peoples"; LOGOS: "his people"; PNEUMA: "their people." SOPHIA rules for "sons of Israel" — the established project convention throughout Genesis and Exodus. (2) Prohibition construction: EMET: "Any fat... you shall not eat" (literal, OV order); LOGOS: "You shall eat no fat" (English negation integrated with verb); PNEUMA: "You must not eat any fat" (modal "must not"). SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "You shall eat no fat" — elegant, clear, and appropriate in register. (3) "ox" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "cattle" (PNEUMA) for shor. SOPHIA follows "ox" (EMET/LOGOS) — shor is the bovine, not generically "cattle." (4) PNEUMA drops "saying" from the introduction; SOPHIA retains it per v. 22 formula.

### Verse 24 — "animal that died of itself" / "animal that dies of itself" / "died naturally"; "torn by beasts" / "torn by beasts" / "killed by wild animals"; "shall certainly not eat it" / "shall by no means eat it" / "must never eat it"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "And the fat of an animal that died and the fat of what was torn may be used for any purpose, but you shall certainly not eat it." |
| LOGOS | "The fat of an animal that dies of itself and the fat of one that is torn by beasts may be put to any other use, but you shall by no means eat it." |
| PNEUMA | "The fat of an animal that has died naturally or been killed by wild animals may be used for any other purpose, but you must never eat it." |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "The fat of an animal that died of itself and the fat of one that is torn by beasts may be put to any other use, but you shall by no means eat it." Differences: (1) EMET opens with "And the fat"; LOGOS and PNEUMA drop "And." SOPHIA drops "And" — a new legal specification, not a continuation of the previous clause. (2) Nevela: EMET "animal that died" (omits "of itself"); LOGOS "dies of itself" (present tense, generic); PNEUMA "died naturally" (adds "naturally"). SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "died of itself" captures the precise legal meaning (natural death, not slaughter) without adding the interpretive "naturally." (3) Terefah: EMET "what was torn" (general); LOGOS "one that is torn by beasts" (specific agent); PNEUMA "killed by wild animals" (different mechanism). SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "torn by beasts" preserves the Hebrew root (taraf = to tear) and specifies the agent. (4) "any purpose" (EMET) vs. "any other use" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) — the Hebrew le-khol melacha means "for any work/task." SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA: "any other use" makes the contrast with eating explicit. (5) "certainly not eat it" (EMET) vs. "by no means eat it" (LOGOS) vs. "never eat it" (PNEUMA) — all render the emphatic infinitive absolute (akhol lo tokhelu). SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "by no means eat it" — a strong prohibition that is somewhat more formal than "never."

**SOPHIA ruling:** "And you shall eat no blood whatever, whether of bird or of animal, in any of your dwelling places." Differences: (1) LOGOS uses "Moreover" for the waw; EMET and PNEUMA use "And." SOPHIA follows EMET/PNEUMA: "And" — "Moreover" is interpretive (the waw is coordinative, not strongly contrastive). (2) "any blood" (EMET/PNEUMA) vs. "no blood whatever" (LOGOS). SOPHIA adopts LOGOS's "no blood whatever" — it more forcefully renders the emphatic kol-dam (any/all blood) with the absolute prohibition tone, and the "whatever" mirrors the "any" of the Hebrew. SOPHIA follows LOGOS but places "in any of your dwelling places" at the end (after the species qualifiers), which is the Hebrew word order (be-khol moshevoteikhem is the last phrase in v. 26). (4) PNEUMA drops "bird or animal" specifics and says "any bird or animal" — losing the two-category distinction. SOPHIA retains "bird or animal."

The Hebrew is chazeh ha-tenufah — a construct phrase meaning "the breast of the tenufah / the wave-offering breast." | PNEUMA | "This is the portion set apart for Aaron and for his sons from the LORD's fire-offerings, from the day they were presented to serve the LORD as priests —" |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "This is the anointed portion of Aaron and the anointed portion of his sons from the fire offerings of the LORD, from the day they were presented to serve as priests to the LORD —" Differences: (1) "anointed portion" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "portion set apart" (PNEUMA) for mishchat. SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: "anointed portion." PNEUMA's "portion set apart" erases the connection to anointing that the Hebrew mishchat encodes. (2) "fire offerings of the LORD" (EMET) vs. "the LORD's fire offerings" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). SOPHIA follows EMET's genitive order. (3) "on the day he presented them" (EMET, single instance of mishchat) vs. "from the day they were presented" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). The Hebrew be-yom haqriv otam could be read as "on the day" (point in time) or "from the day" (starting point). SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA: "from the day" — the priestly rights flow perpetually from the inaugural moment. (4) "to minister as priests to the LORD" (EMET) vs. "to serve as priests for the LORD" (LOGOS) vs. "to serve the LORD as priests" (PNEUMA). SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "to serve as priests to the LORD" — slightly more formal than PNEUMA's order, and "to" is the appropriate preposition for service directed toward a person.

---

### Verse 36 — "from the sons of Israel" / "from the people of Israel" / omitted; "perpetual statute" / "perpetual statute" / "permanent obligation"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "Which the LORD commanded to give them on the day he anointed them from the sons of Israel — a perpetual statute throughout their generations." |
| LOGOS | "which the LORD commanded to be given them, from the day that he anointed them, from the people of Israel. It is a perpetual statute throughout their generations." |
| PNEUMA | "which the LORD commanded to be given them from the Israelites on the day he anointed them. This is a permanent obligation throughout their generations." |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "which the LORD commanded to be given them from the sons of Israel, on the day that he anointed them. It is a perpetual statute throughout their generations." Differences: (1) "sons of Israel" vs. "people of Israel" vs. "Israelites" — SOPHIA follows "sons of Israel." (2) "to give them" (EMET) vs. "to be given them" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). SOPHIA follows LOGOS/PNEUMA: "to be given them" — the passive renders latet lahem appropriately in a divine-command context. (3) "on the day he anointed them" (EMET/PNEUMA) vs. "from the day that he anointed them" (LOGOS). SOPHIA follows EMET/PNEUMA: "on the day" — be-yom mosho otam is a specific point in time (the day of anointing), not an ongoing period. (4) "perpetual statute" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "permanent obligation" (PNEUMA). SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: "perpetual statute." (5) LOGOS ends with "It is a perpetual statute"; EMET has "a perpetual statute throughout their generations" (appended nominally); PNEUMA has "This is a permanent obligation." SOPHIA renders it as LOGOS but with EMET's full phrase: "It is a perpetual statute throughout their generations."

---

### Verse 37 — Chattat as "sin offering" vs. "purification offering" again in the colophon

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "This is the torah of the burnt offering, the grain offering, the sin offering, the guilt offering, the ordination offering, and the sacrifice of peace offerings," |
| LOGOS | "This is the instruction of the burnt offering, of the grain offering, of the sin offering, of the guilt offering, of the ordination offering, and of the sacrifice of peace offerings," |
| PNEUMA | "This, then, is the procedure for the burnt offering, the grain offering, the purification offering, the guilt offering, the ordination offering, and the peace offering —" |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "This is the instruction of the burnt offering, of the grain offering, of the sin offering, of the guilt offering, of the ordination offering, and of the sacrifice of peace offerings," Four differences: (1) "torah" (EMET) vs. "instruction" (LOGOS) vs. "procedure" (PNEUMA). SOPHIA follows "instruction" (LOGOS), the project convention. (2) "sin offering" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "purification offering" (PNEUMA). SOPHIA follows "sin offering" — project convention. (3) LOGOS includes the repeated preposition "of" before each offering type (following the Hebrew la-olah ve-la-minchah ve-la-chattat...); EMET uses the preposition only for the first, then lists with commas; PNEUMA omits prepositions. SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "of the burnt offering, of the grain offering..." — the repeated "of" in each construct is present in the Hebrew and provides formal colophon rhythm. (4) "sacrifice of peace offerings" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "peace offering" (PNEUMA). SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS — the full technical term belongs in the colophon.

---

### Verse 38 — "sons of Israel" / "people of Israel" / "Israelites"; "bring their offerings" / "offer their offerings" / "present their offerings"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "which the LORD commanded Moses on Mount Sinai, on the day he commanded the sons of Israel to offer their offerings to the LORD in the wilderness of Sinai." |
| LOGOS | "which the LORD commanded Moses on Mount Sinai, on the day that he commanded the people of Israel to bring their offerings to the LORD, in the wilderness of Sinai." |
| PNEUMA | "which the LORD commanded Moses at Mount Sinai on the day he commanded the Israelites to present their offerings to the LORD, in the wilderness of Sinai." |

**SOPHIA ruling:** "which the LORD commanded Moses on Mount Sinai, on the day that he commanded the sons of Israel to bring their offerings to the LORD, in the wilderness of Sinai." Differences: (1) "sons of Israel" vs. "people of Israel" vs. "Israelites" — SOPHIA follows "sons of Israel." (2) "on Mount Sinai" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "at Mount Sinai" (PNEUMA). The Hebrew be-har sinai uses the preposition be- ("in/on/at"). SOPHIA follows "on Mount Sinai" (EMET/LOGOS) — "on the mountain" is the established English idiom. (3) "offer their offerings" (EMET) vs. "bring their offerings" (LOGOS) vs. "present their offerings" (PNEUMA) for le-haqriv et-qorbanehem. SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "bring their offerings" — "to bring offerings" is natural English for haqriv, which in certain contexts is better rendered "bring" (the hiphil of qarav meaning "to bring near/to present"). (4) LOGOS adds "that" in "on the day that he commanded" — SOPHIA retains "that" for clarity.

| Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA |
|-------|------|-------|--------|--------|
| torah | "instruction" | "instruction" | "procedure" | **"instruction"** (project convention) |
| chattat | "sin offering" | "sin offering" | "purification offering" | **"sin offering"** (project convention) |
| bney yisrael | "sons of Israel" | "people of Israel" | "Israelites" | **"sons of Israel"** (project convention) |
| zaraq (v. 2) | "dash" | "thrown" | "splashed" | **"thrown"** |
| piggul (v. 18) | transliterated | "abomination" | "offensive" | **"piggul"** (technical term preserved) |
| avon (v. 18) | "iniquity" | "iniquity" | "guilt" | **"iniquity"** |
| nefesh in legal contexts | "life" | "person" | "person" | **"person"** |
| pronoun for nefesh | feminine "her" | masculine "his" | plural "their" | **masculine "his"** |
| chuqat olam | "perpetual due" | "perpetual statute" | "permanent obligation/share" | **"perpetual statute"** |
| tenufah gesture | "wave" | "waved" | "elevated" | **"waved"** |
| mishchat | "anointed portion" | "anointed portion" | "portion set apart" | **"anointed portion"** |
| basar | "flesh" | "flesh" | "meat" | **"flesh"** |
